coredata.dc:title: A first principles approach to the interactions of alkali metal atoms with carbon quantum dots

coredata.dc:creator: Politi, J.R.S.
Martins, J.B.L.
Cabral, B.J.C.
coredata.dc:description:
We investigate the interactions between alkali metal atoms (M = Li,Na,K) and carbon quantum dot models (CQDs) by density functional theory. For systems where Li and Na metal atoms are initially placed between two nearly planar CQDs, the interaction with the metal species induces a significant reorganization of the carbon network with the formation of macrocycles, which keep some similarity with fragments of carbon nanotubes. Binding energies of metal atoms to CQDs are significantly stronger than what is observed in pristine graphene and also in graphene nanoribbons. CQDs are characterized by high electron affinities. For a model with 32 carbon atoms [
CQD
32
] the first and second electron affinities are 4.3 and 2.1 eV, respectively. Time dependent density functional (TDDFT) calculations for the electronic spectra of [CQD-M] complexes are compared with experimental information for similar carbon based structures. The results relying on TDDFT for [CQD-M] indicate that the enhancement of the absorption in the visible region in comparison with isolated [CQD] is related to excitations with a significant charge-transfer character.

head.ce:title: A first principles approach to the interactions of alkali metal atoms with carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
We investigate the interactions between alkali metal atoms (M = Li,Na,K) and carbon quantum dot models (CQDs) by density functional theory. For systems where Li and Na metal atoms are initially placed between two nearly planar CQDs, the interaction with the metal species induces a significant reorganization of the carbon network with the formation of macrocycles, which keep some similarity with fragments of carbon nanotubes. Binding energies of metal atoms to CQDs are significantly stronger than what is observed in pristine graphene and also in graphene nanoribbons. CQDs are characterized by high electron affinities. For a model with 32 carbon atoms [
CQD
32
] the first and second electron affinities are 4.3 and 2.1 eV, respectively. Time dependent density functional (TDDFT) calculations for the electronic spectra of [CQD-M] complexes are compared with experimental information for similar carbon based structures. The results relying on TDDFT for [CQD-M] indicate that the enhancement of the absorption in the visible region in comparison with isolated [CQD] is related to excitations with a significant charge-transfer character.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Electronic absorption spectra and charge transfer

ce:section.ce:para: The design and synthesis of carbon based nanostructures with a wide spectrum of applications in materials science [1,2], biomedicine [3], chemical sensing [4,5], photocatalysis [6], artificial photosynthesis [7] is presently a major challenge. The diversity of carbon nanoallotropes [8,9] opens several promising routes. Two related systems of strong interest are graphene quantum dots [10–15] (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [16–25]. Quantum dots are characterized by quantum effects related to finite size at the nanoscale. These effects lead to the dependence of the electronic properties on the size and shape of the nanoparticles [26] that can be tuned to achieve specific purposes such as the control of optical/fundamental gaps and photoemission spectra. GQDs are defined, in general, by a sp2 carbon network similar to graphene, where doping and surface functionalization play a fundamental role. CQDs can be considered more complex carbon nanostructures with a hybrid sp-sp2 network [27]. They can be formed by aggregates of carbon building blocks. The aggregates are nearly spherical with a polydisperse size distribution. Doping [28,22] and functionalization at the CQDs surface is of great interest making possible the tuning of their optical properties as well as the solubility in water and other non-aqueous solvents [18]. Moreover, it appears that the photoluminescence (PL) of CQDs relies on absorption/emission processes from the carbon framework itself and can be tuned by size effects [17]. Interesting discussions on the adopted terminology for identifying carbon based nanostructures can be found in recent review articles [19,21].

In contrast with GQDs for which several theoretical works have been carried out [29–35], much less is known concerning fundamental aspects of CQDs. Specific issues are the structure of CQDs and their interactions with metal atoms, radical and molecular species, the dynamics and energetics associated with aggregation, and the electronic properties. Here, focus will be placed on the interactions between CQD models and alkali metal atoms (M = Li,Na,K). Several works on the interactions between metal atoms and carbon nanostructures were reported [36,37]. Banhart [38] presented a review on metal–carbon nanotubes interaction. Chepkasov et al. [39] reported an article on alkali metals inside bi-layer graphene. Mikhailov et al. [40] investigated the interactions of cations of alkali metals with a fragment of graphene in the benzene environment [40]. In what concern the importance of CQD-M interactions, Li et al. have shown that an alkaline environment combined with the presence of OH− groups is a key factor in the formation of CQDs [17]. More recently, Hassan et al. [41] discussed the importance of chemical activation with KOH for the preparation of GQDs with both free and bound edges [41]. A review article by Tian et al. [25] pointed out the fundamental interest in the understanding of CQD metal ions coordination for optimizing the role played by CQDs in electrocatalysis [25]. These works underline the interest in the understanding of CQD-M interactions.

The presently adopted methodology relies on density functional theory (DFT) [42] and time dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) [43]. After presenting computational details, we discuss the structure of the CQD models and CQD-M complexes. Then, we investigate the energetics of the interactions, the adiabatic ionization energies and electron affinities of CQD and CQD-M. We also analyse the structural changes of CQD aggregates induced by the interactions with metal species, such as the formation of macrocycles similar to “carbon baskets”. In the last part of the article we discuss the electronic absorption spectra of CQD-M complexes with emphasis on the changes relative to isolated CQDs.

Geometry optimizations for complexes of alkali metals (M = Li, Na, K) with carbon quantum dots were carried out with the CP2K program [44] that adopts a mixed gaussian and plane wave (GPW) [45] basis-set method. PBE combined with the Grimme empirical correction for dispersion energy (D3) [46] was used in all the geometry optimizations, which were carried out with the triple-
ζ
(TZVP-MOLOPT-GTH) [47] for carbon atoms, and double-
ζ
short-range (DZVP-MOLOPT-SR-GTH) [47] basis-sets for the metal species. Goedecker-Tetter-Hutter (GTH) [48] pseudopotentials optimized [49] for the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) [50] exchange–correlation functional as implemented in CP2K were employed. Geometry optimizations based on the PBE[GTH] approach were carried out with the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) [51] optimizer. The Martyna-Tuckerman (MT) procedure [52] for non-periodic systems was applied. A cutoff of 400 Ry and a relative cutoff of 40 Ry were adopted. The self-consistent field (SCF) energy threshold was 10−6 hartree. We defined carbon aggregates with different number n of carbon atoms (
n = 32 , 64
) for modelling CQDs. These systems are represented by [
CQD
n
]. Different complexes with alkali metal atoms (M) are investigated including the neutral [
CQD
n
-M], cationic [
CQD
n
-M]+, and anionic [
CQD
n
-M]− species, which were fully optimized. We also discuss the structure and electronic properties of neutral systems where two metal atoms interact with
CQD
64
, which was defined by stacking two
CQD
32
building blocks. We observe that it is important to discuss stacking in CQD models since this is a main feature of other carbon-systems such as graphene. For complexes with metal species, the initial structure is defined by two
CQD
32
layers, where the metal atoms are placed between the layers. They will be represented as [
CQD
n
-M k ], where
n = 64
and
k = 1 , 2
. A box with dimensions of
25 ( x ) × 25 ( y ) × 15 ( z )
Å was used in all the calculations. We report the optimized neutral structures the cartesian coordinates as Supplementary Material. We investigate electronic absorption by time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) [43]. Excitation energies were calculated with PBE and also with the long-range corrected PBE (LC-wPBE) [53] by using PBE[GTH] optimized geometries. In LC-wPBE calculations w = 0.4 bohr−1 was adopted [53]. We notice, however, the possibility of tuning w for a more accurate prediction of excitation energies [54,55] and for a better description of charge localization and minimization of spin contamination in unrestricted open-shell calculations (ULC-wPBE) [56]. Excitation energies were calculated with all the electrons. The Dunning’s double-
ζ
correlation-consistent basis-set (cc-pVDZ) [57] was used in the all-electron calculations, which were carried out with the Gaussian-09 program [58]. For the calculation of the electronic absorption spectra the number of excited states (
N
s
) was taken as
N
s
= 400
(
n = 32
) and
N
s
= 250
(
n = 64
).

In contrast with the regular carbon network of [
GQD
30
], the CQD model illustrates the presence of different all-carbon hybridization schemes [27] including sp2, which characterizes the GQD network. Some nearly linear arrangement of carbon atoms, which are typical of sp hybridization are also found. C–C bond distances range from 1.25 Å to 1.45 Å (the cartesian coordinates of the optimized structures are reported as Supplementary Material). A triangular arrangement of carbon atoms can be observed at the
CQD
32
lower edges and the different C–C distances are reported in Fig. 1. It should be observed that the present CQD models are characterized by incomplete valence of carbon atoms. This feature keeps some similarity with graphite, where delocalised (free) electrons lead to a high electrical conductivity [59]. Notice the presence of both armchair (upper side) and zigzag (lateral sides) arrangements along the edges of the CQD model. The C–C bond distances in [
GQD
30
] are in the 1.39–1.47 Å range. The first value is similar to the C–C distance benzene, whereas 1.47 Å is 0.5 Å above the value in the sp2 network of graphene. The smallest distance (1.39 Å) reflects edge effects in [
GQD
30
]. The [
CQD
64
] system is defined by two [
CQD
32
] fragments in a displaced face-to-face configuration, which is energetically stabilized by dispersion interactions.

Fig. 2 illustrates the binding modes of a metallic species (M) to [
CQD
32
]. In the first binding mode (BM1), the metal atom is above the plane defined by the hexagonal carbon ring and interacts with the six closest carbon atoms. In the second one (BM2), M interacts with the two nearest carbon atoms, at the upper edge (armchair arrangement) of the [
CQD
32
] model. It is not our purpose to fully exploit the possible binding modes of our CQD model with M atoms. However, it should be expected that binding to the lateral zigzag arrangement is also possible and some results for a third (BM3) are reported. In addition, we investigated the possibility of binding a metal to a carbon atom at the edge of the triangular arrangement. This mode is named BM4 (see Fig. 2) and was only investigated for the Na atom.

We report data for the structure of [
CQD
32
-M] complexes in Table 1 . For M=(Li,Na) two binding modes (BM1 and BM2) were found. For BM1 we report the distance between the metal species and the center of the carbon ring (X). For BM2 (BM3) the distances are between the metal and the carbon atoms at the upper (side) edges of the CQD model as indicated in Fig. 2.

For the neutral [
CQD
32
-M] complexes and BM1 the M-X distances are 1.83 Å and 2.40 Å for Li and Na, respectively. However, BM1 was not observed in [
CQD
32
-K]. A possible explanation is that for BM1 the interaction between K and the nearest carbon atoms is not favoured due to relative size of K (van der Waals radius of 2.7 Å) and the hexagonal carbon ring (2.86 Å).

For cationic [
CQD
32
-M]+ species (M=Li,Na) and BM1 a small increase of the M-X distance is observed in comparison with neutral systems, whereas in the anionic [
CQD
32
-Li]− complex a small decrease of M-X relative to the neutral system is observed. Although the differences between the M-X distances for the neutral and charged complexes are small they should reflect charge transfer and polarization effects in the CQD framework. A simple analysis relying on a Mulliken localization scheme for [
CQD
32
-Li] shows that the negative charges of the carbon atoms in closest interaction with the metal increases relative to the neutral system for the anionic system, whereas a decrease is observed for the cationic species. A similar trend (increase of M-X relative to the neutral species in the cation and decrease in the anion) is observed for BM2, a trend that in this case concerns the M-C interaction. For BM3 the two closest carbon atoms are not equivalent and two different M-C distances are observed. In BM4 the metal is interacting with only one carbon atom at the edge of a triangular arrangement.

Further insight on other possible binding modes can be inferred by analyzing the electrostatic potential, that is here represented over an isodensity surface. The isodensity surface and the corresponding electrostatic potential are shown in Fig. 3 for isolated [
CQD
32
] and also for the neutral, cationic, and anionic BM2 of [
CQD
32
-Na].

The cutoff value for the electronic density [0.08
e /
Å3] places the isodensity surface close to the CQD framework. The electrostatic potential of isolated [
CQD
32
] is characterized by alternating zones of higher (close to carbon atoms) and lower (in the C–C bond regions) values. Excepting a small decrease of the electrostatic potential in the region closer to the upper edge of CQD that interacts with the metal no significant differences are observed when we compare [
CQD
32
] and the neutral [
CQD
32
-Na] complex. Electron attachment to [CQD-M] leads to a significant decrease of the electrostatic potential and the negative regions close to the metal atoms are in keeping with a decrease of the M-C distance in the BM2 of [
CQD
32
-Na]− (see Table 1).

The starting point for the optimization of the [
CQD
64
-M k ] (
k
=1,2) was chosen by placing the metal atoms between two face-to-face [
CQD
32
] fragments. This is illustrated for [
CQD
64
-Li] in Fig. 4 , where the optimized structures of [
CQD
64
-M1] (M=Li,Na,K) are shown. For [
CQD
64
-Li] the Li atom is placed between two displaced face-to-face [
CQD
64
] fragments. The Li atom interacts preferentially with one of the fragments at a distance of 1.79 Å from the center of the carbon ring and the final configuration indicates that the BM1 (see Fig. 2) plays a dominant role in this case. The distance between the Li atom and the closest carbon atom of the second fragment is 2.30 Å. The optimized structure of [
CQD
64
-Na] is shown in the middle panel of Fig. 4. In this case, there is a significant reorganization of the CQD geometry and the Na atom now interacts with the two closest carbon atoms in a configuration similar to the BM1. The closest distances between the Na and carbon atoms (indicated in Fig. 4) are 2.42 and 2.72 Å. We stress again that the starting structure for the optimization of [
CQD
64
-Na] was similar to the [
CQD
64
-Li] shown in the left panel of Fig. 4. Therefore, the reorganization of the CQD framework induced by the interaction with Na, reflects a possible competition between the two binding modes BM1 and BM2 shown in Fig. 2 and the final structure shows an exo-complexation. The right panel of Fig. 4 shows the optimized structure of [
CQD
64
-K], which keeps some similarity with that one for [
CQD
64
-Na] and indicates that alkali metal atoms may lead to a significant reorganization of the CQDs carbon network.

We show the optimized structures for [
CQD
64
-M2] in Fig. 5 . A few geometric parameters are also presented (see Supplementary Material for all the cartesian coordinates). For both Li and Na atoms, geometry optimization leads to carbon macrocycles. In these cases, one of the atoms is well inside the macrocycle (endo-complexation), whereas the second one is outside. These structures seem to reflect the competition between the binding modes BM1 and BM2 previously discussed for the [
CQD
32
-M] systems. Interestingly, the results suggest the building up of carbon macrocycles through the interaction of CQDs with metal atoms. The distance between the Li atoms in the complex (3.17 Å) is larger than the experimental value for the Li2 dimer (2.67 Å). The BE for the Li2 dimer is 0.86 eV, which is 1.03 eV below the BE of Li in the BM1 of [
CQD
32
-Li]. A similar trend is observed in [
CQD
64
-Na2], where the Na-Na distance is 3.43 Å, which is in this case larger than the experimental equilibrium distance (3.08 Å) in the dimer [60]. The BE of Na2 predicted by PBE (0.75 eV) is quite close to experiment and 0.87 eV below the BE in the BM1 of the [
CQD
32
-Na] complex. These results indicate that CQD-M interactions are stronger than M-M interactions. The [
CQD
64
-Na2] complex shows a carbon macrocycle in a symmetric
C
2 v
configuration. The distances related to BM2 (2.43 Å) are the same observed in [
CQD
32
-Na], whereas a distance of 3.08 Å is observed between the Na atoms and the center of the closest carbon ring. This is significantly larger than the distance observed in the BM1 for the [
CQD
32
-Na] system (2.43 Å) and seems to reflect a competition between the interactions of Na with two carbon rings.

In the case of potassium, only exo-complexation associated with BM2 is observed. This is in keeping with the results for [
CQD
32
-K] where only BM2 was defined. The K-C distances are quite close to the one observed in [
CQD
32
-K] (2.81 Å). Moreover, the optimized structure shows two [
CQD
32
] moieties in interaction and no significant structural differences are observed when comparison is made with the isolated [
CQD
64
] system. This result suggests a selectivity towards Li and Na for the formation of carbon macrocycles in [
CQD
64
-M2] systems. This selectivity seems to be mainly related to size effects. Other relevant issue is the stability of the generated structures after removing metals from the complexes. This is illustrated in Fig. 5 where we show the new [
CQD
64
] carbon macrocycle after delitihation or removal of Li atoms from [
CQD
64
-Li2]. The role played by the CQD-M interactions for the generation of new carbon networks/macrocycles keeps some similarity with the template effect for the building of macrocycles in supramolecular chemistry [61], particularly in the case of alkali metals and for tuning the textural and structural properties of carbon networks [62].

Here, focus will be placed on the calculation of [
CQD
32
-M] binding energies (BEs), which are reported in Table 2 . This option is justified by the interest in providing a comparison with available data for related carbon based structures..

We adopt two different procedures for estimating BEs. In the first one, the BE energy of [
CQD
32
-M] is calculated as BE = E[
CQD
32
] + E[M] -E[
CQD
32
-M], where E[
CQD
32
-M] is the energy in the fully relaxed optimized structure of the isolated CQD fragment. In the second one, the binding energy (BE-NR) was calculated by taking the geometry of [
CQD
32
-M] in the complexes. The importance of basis-set superposition energies (BE-NR+BSSE) was assessed by using the second procedure, and with the presently adopted basis-sets they amount to less than 0.03 eV.

Comparison between BE and BE-NR shows significant differences and therefore indicates that the CQD structure is modified upon complexation. Interestingly, quite similar differences are observed for the different metals and binding modes. For example, the difference between BE and BE-NR for BM1 are 1.6 eV (Li), 1.7 eV (Na). For BM2 the differences are 2.04 eV (Li), 2.1 eV (Na), and 2.0 eV (K). Comparison between the BE for BM1 and BM2 shows that BM2 is more stable than BM1 by 1.86 eV (Li) and 1.42 eV (Na). For BM3, BEs are smaller than those for BM2 by 0.35 eV (Li), 0.29 eV (Na) and 0.1 eV (K). For BM4 the BE for the Na atom (2.53 eV) is 0.5 eV below BM2.

We are not aware of data for BEs of alkali metals with CQDs. Therefore, comparison will be made with data for BEs with graphene systems. A study of Na on the graphene surface by Malyi et al. [63] pointed out that the most energetically stable position corresponds to the hole site, a configuration where Na interacts with six carbon atoms. This configuration is the same that is presently named as BM1. Their PBE-D2, where D2 is the Grimme’s (D2) semiempirical correction to the dispersion energy [64] predicted a binding energy of 1.14 eV [63], which is 0.48 eV below our estimate (1.62 eV) for the BM1 of [
CQD
32
-Na]. The BM1 configuration was also found as the more stable one in a study by Olsson et al. [65]. In this case the predicted binding energy was 1.22 eV [65]. In a study of alkali metals adsorption on a single graphene sheet, Jin et al. [66] observed that at the limit of very small coverage of the graphene surface, the adsorption energies can be estimated as
1.8
eV (Li) and 1.2 eV (Na). These values are smaller than the present ones but exhibit the same trend BE(Li)
>
BE(Na) for BM1. More recently, Heath and Kuroda [67] reported results for the adsorption energies of alkali metals on a graphene surface. Their value for Li (1.42 eV) is
~ 0.5
eV below our present estimate for the CQD model. For Na our value (1.62 eV) is 0.7 eV below their prediction. A study by Uthaisar et al. [68] discussed the interaction between Li and zigzag graphene nanoribbons (GNRs). The authors observed that the interaction is stronger by 50% than in graphene. They have also predicted that the interaction of Li with zigzag GNRs is stronger than with armchair GNRs. For zigzag GNRs their PBE values are in the 1.3–1.7 eV range depending on the position of the hexagonal lattice with which Li is interacting. Therefore, they should be compared with BEs for BM1. Although their larger value (1.7 eV) is only 0.13 eV below the present estimate for the BE of Li, it is important to notice that a direct comparison is made difficult due to the differences between the two systems and also by the absence of dispersion corrections in the results reported by Uthaisar et al. [68]. Here, what is more important is to focus on the role played by edge effects. It is expected that they lead to an increase in the binding energies relative to pristine graphene [68] and also change the metal diffusion on the surface of the carbon aggregates [69]. More recently Sun et al. [70] discussed the adsorption and diffusion of sodium on graphene with grain boundaries. They predicted BEs in the 0.79–1.32 eV, significantly higher than the adsorption energy of Na on the surface of pristine graphene (0.67 eV). Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that our results support these findings. However, we are also providing evidence that binding of alkali metals to the edges of a CQD model is still stronger than what it was observed on the surface of GNRs.

We report adiabatic ionization energies (IEs) and electron affinities (EAs) for [
CQD
32
-M] complexes in Table 3 . We are also reporting results for the [
GQD
30
] and [
CQD
32
] models. The results rely on
Δ E
(SCF) calculations or in the difference between the energies of charged and neutral species at their respective optimized geometries. We are not reporting adiabatic IEs and EAs for [
CQD
64
-M2] systems. One reason is the high computational cost involved in the geometry optimizations of the related charged species.

The results for the [
GQD
30
] model can be compared with data from the literature for similar graphene systems. Setianto et al. [71] reported results for the ionization energies and electron affinities for some GQD models. Their results, relying on semiempirical calculations predict IEs in the 5.3–7.2 eV range, where the lower value is for
C
22
H
12
and the higher for
C
18
H
12
. EAs ranged from 0.24 eV (
C
18
H
12
) to 2.65 eV (
C
22
H
12
) [71]. It is known that carbon based systems such as fullerenes exhibit a higher electron affinity [72] with an experimental value of 2.65
±
0.05 eV [73]. Liu et al. [24] reported results for the ionization energies and electron affinities of polydisperse flame-formed carbon nanoparticles. Their results indicate that the ionization energy is dependent on the average particle size that they define in terms of an average mobility diameter. However, the electron affinity is significantly less dependent on this size related property [24]. They predict electrochemical ionization energies in the
~ 5 - 6
eV range depending on the particle size, whereas the electron affinities are close to
~ 4.6
eV. Although we are not aware of experimental results for IEs and EAs of carbon quantum dots, the present result for [
CQD
32
] (6.7 eV and 4.3 eV) are in line with it is observed for related carbon based systems. Our high value (4.3 eV) for the [
CQD
32
] EA, indicates, in keeping with results for fullerenes [72,74], that multiple electron attachment should be also observed in CQDs. Actually, the second EA affinity of [
CQD
32
] is presently calculated as 2.1 eV at the PBE level, a value which is quite close to the first EA of [
GQD
30
]. Although multiple electron attachment is related to the role played by carbon based structures as strong electron acceptors, a comprehensive discussion on this subject is outside the scope of the present study. Although the valence orbitals are mainly localized on the CQD framework the ordering of the IEs of CQD-M for both BM1 and BM2 is the same observed in the experimental IEs (in eV) for alkali metals: IE(Li) = 5.39 > IE(Na) = 5.10 > IE(K) = 4.31. However, it should be observed that in comparison with CQD, IEs of CQD-M are higher, which should be related with the energetic stabilization of the outer valence orbitals upon complexation with the metal species. Therefore, the IEs ordering in CQD-M should also reflect the role played by the interaction with the polarization field of the metal atoms. IE-EA, or the hardness parameter
η
for the open-shell complexes are increased by [1.8 eV (K)–2.1 eV (Li)] when compared with the H-L gap of [
CQD
32
].

We report the electronic absorption spectra of [
CQD
32
] and [
CQD
32
-M] (M=Li,Na) in Fig. 6 ..

For these systems, we identified two binding modes (BM1 and BM2), whereas only BM2 was found for [
CQD
32
-K]. We are only reporting results for Li and Na. It is reasonable to assume that for K the changes relative to [
CQD
32
] are similar to what is observed in BM2 for complexes with Li and Na. The electronic absorption spectrum of GQDs was investigated by several works [75–79] and it is one of the most important aspects of quantum dots. Here, we will focus on: (1) the comparison between the present theoretical results for [
CQD
32
] with experimental data for related models [75,77,22,79]; (2) the analysis of the changes in the absorption spectrum of [
CQD
32
] induced by the interactions with the metal atoms. However, comparison between the present results for CQD models with experiment is not direct. Firstly, UV–vis experiments are, in many cases, carried out in solution [75]. Moreover, the molecular structure of several carbon based models are not exactly the same presently investigated, although it is expected that the changes in the experimental spectra due to solvent or substituent effects, make still possible, at least, a qualitative comparison with the present models.

The absorption spectrum of carbon nanocrystals in water were reported by Zhou et al. [75]. The spectrum is characterized by a first band at 270 nm, although a shoulder at
~
220 nm is apparently also present. Absorption (ABS) and photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs derived from graphene oxide were investigated by Zhu et al. [76]. The UV–vis spectrum exhibited a decaying behaviour with increasing wavenumber (decreasing energy) with a weak shoulder at 320 nm [76]. ABS and PL of mono-dispersed disk-like GQDs of
~ 60
nm diameter and 2–3 nm thickness were investigated by Liu et al. [77]. Again, the absorption spectrum was characterized by a fast decay regime and a weak shoulder at
~ 280
nm, in agreement with experimental results for chemically reduced graphene [80]. A similar behaviour was observed by Yang et al. [78] in their study of fluorescence properties of GQDs and their dependence on the pH. In this case, the weak shoulders in the absorption spectrum were observed at
~ 270
nm [78]. More recently, Feng et al. [79] carried out TD-DFT for GQDs in water by using a polarizable continuum model (PCM) [81]. They pointed out the presence of a well defined band with a maximum at 363 nm in very good agreement with an experimental study on the absorption spectra of polycyclic hydrocarbons (360 nm) [82].

The absorption spectra of CQDs was discussed by Lin et al. [22]. They pointed out that the main experimental features are (1) in CQDs: an absorption maximum in the UV region (200–300 nm); (2) in CQD-M systems (here, M denotes a metal in general and not only alkali metals): strong and broad absorption in the 200–600 nm [22]. Therefore, at least qualitatively, the main difference between CQDs and CQD-M is that absorption is extended to the visible range (380 nm-750 nm) in the complexes. Moreover, a maximum at
~ 225
nm is consistent with
π
∗
← π
transitions typical of C=C in sp2 networks [22].

For the ABS spectrum of [
CQD
32
] our PBE calculations show a first band with a maximum at
~ 215
nm, a weak shoulder at 290 nm, and a third maximum around 387 nm. Comparison with LC-wPBE shows some differences. The first one is the presence of a well defined first band at
~ 225
nm and a second band with a maximum at 270 nm. Comparison with experimental results, specifically with those reported by Zhou et al. [75], supports the LC-wPBE predictions that will be taken as reference for discussing the ABS spectra. The long-range corrected exchange correlation functional also predicts a third wide band with a maximum at 400 nm, which is
~ 40
nm above the experimental results for polycyclic hydrocarbons (360 nm) [79].

From LC-wPBE calculations, comparison between the absorption spectra [
CQD
32
] and [
CQD
32
-Na] (BM1) suggests that they are similar, excepting for the positions of third maxima which are at 400 nm [
CQD
32
] and 450 nm for the complex with Na. However, some differences are observed for BM2. In this case, only a weak shoulder at
~ 261
nm is observed in contrast with the maxima at 270 nm for [
CQD
32
]. Moreover, a broad band at
~
520 nm is now present.

Comparison between the absorption spectra of [
CQD
32
-Li] (BM1) and [
CQD
32
] also indicates that they are similar. The only difference concerns the maxima of the third absorption band, which is displaced from 400 to 460 nm, thus indicating a
~
0.4 eV red-shift from [
CQD
32
] to [
CQD
32
-Li] (BM1). For BM2 the main difference between the spectra of [
CQD
32
] and [
CQD
32
-Li] is the absence of a well defined third absorption band in the complex, whereas [
CQD
32
] shows a maximum at 400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Electronic absorption of [
CQD
64
-M k ] (M=Li,Na; k = 1,2)
ce:section.ce:para:
We show the electronic absorption spectra of [
CQD
64
-M k ] (M=Li,Na; k = 1,2) in Fig. 7 where we also report the results for [
CQD
32
] and [
CQD
64
].

Comparison between the spectra of [
CQD
32
] and [
CQD
64
] shows that the maxima of the [
CQD
32
] spectrum at 270 nm is now only a shoulder in [
CQD
64
]. This result is interesting because it indicates that
π
-stacking that characterizes [
CQD
64
] (see Fig. 4) may have some influence on the ABS spectra of CQDs.

Comparison between ABS spectra of [
CQD
64
-Li] and [
CQD
64
-Li2] suggests that higher energy absorption (small wavelengths) is significantly smaller for [
CQD
64
-Li], which shows a broad first band with a maximum at
~ 280
nm. The [
CQD
64
-Li2] spectrum shows a first maximum at
~ 225
nm followed by a broad band with a maximum near 290 nm. Moreover, the spectrum of [
CQD
64
-Li] indicates small absorption beyond 600 nm, whereas for [
CQD
64
-Li2] a maximum at nearly 620 nm is observed. The spectrum of [
CQD
64
-Na1] shows a first maximum at 242 nm and a shoulder close to 355 nm. The spectrum of [
CQD
64
-Na2] indicates high energy absorption close to 210 nm, a maximum at
~
280 nm and a third low energy band around 500 nm.

Weak energy absorption for (
λ > 500
) nm is in general observed in the isolated [
CQD
n
], (
n = 1 , 2
) models. Actually, energy absorption in the visible region seems to be a fingerprint of complexation of CQDs with metals [22]. In the present case, this trend (absorption beyond 400 nm) is observed for all the [CQD-M2] complexes, which is in keeping with experimental information [22]. However, it appears that a more complex picture for understanding specific features of the [CQD-M] ABS spectra is involved. Actually, in some cases, as for example [
CQD
64
-M1] (M=Li,Na) absorption at low energies (beyond 500 nm) is not observed. Therefore, a better understanding of the [CQD-M] structures and their relationship with the ABS spectra still deserves further analysis.
Fig. 8 shows the frontier orbitals (HOMO=H, SOMO=S, and LUMO=L) as well as some additional orbitals that were chosen based on the contribution they give to the maxima in the ABS spectra.

For [
CQD
32
] (top panels of Fig. 8) the H is a
π
orbital and L is
σ
∗
. For isolated [
CQD
32
] the L
σ
∗
← π
H low energy transition is forbidden by symmetry. However, it should be observed that low energy excitations with very small oscillator strengths can be enhanced by solvent effects making difficult a comparison with experiment that are carried out in solution [75]. In these cases, solvent effects need to be taken into account in theoretical calculations [83]. The two other panels represent the virtual orbitals L + 2 and L + 10, which significantly contribute to the observed maxima in the ABS spectra at 400 nm and 225 nm, and are related to
π
∗
← π
transitions. We notice that for L + 2 and L + 10 the wavefunctions representation is in keeping with a significant electronic density reorganization in comparison with H, thus suggesting a charge transfer (CT) transitions from the HOMO. In the middle panels of Fig. 8 we represent the orbitals for [
CQD
64
-Na]. In addition to the SOMO, the LUMO, L + 2, and L + 6 are also shown. The L + 2
←
S and L + 6
←
S transitions significantly contribute to the maxima of the ABS spectrum at 355 nm and 244 nm, respectively, although other transitions are also involved.

The bottom panels of Fig. 8 show the H, L, L + 4, and L + 27 of [
CQD
64
-Na2]. The L + 4
←
H transition contributes to the maximum of the ABS spectrum at 500 nm, whereas the L + 27
←
H transition is associated with the maximum at 225 nm. It is not our purpose to detail all relevant contributions for each one of the ABS maxima. It is more important to discuss the nature of the transitions. In this sense, in keeping with the results for [
CQD
32
] it appears that electronic density reorganization with charge localization is also present in the [CQD-Na] excitations. This support our choice of a long-range corrected exchange–correlation functional for addressing electronic absorption in CQD and CQD-M. Finally, as pointed out by Lin et al. [22], the enhancement of the CQD-M absorption in the visible can related be to CT excitations [22]. Our results provide additional support to this interpretation.

DFT calculations predict that binding energies between CQDs and alkali metal atoms are stronger than what is observed in GQDs and graphene nanoribbons. Moreover, CQD-M BEs are similar for M=(Li,Na,K). The CQD-M interactions lead to a significant reorganization of the CQDs carbon network. The generation of carbon macrocycles strongly indicates the interesting possibility of designing new carbon networks by the formation of CQD-M complexes. This conclusion is supported by experimental results in materials science [62] and supramolecular chemistry [61] where alkali metal atoms play the role of template agents for generating macrocycles [61].

For the open-shell [
CQD
32
-M] (M = Li,Na,K) complexes, IEs exhibit some dependence with M. Their ordering is the same observed in isolated alkali metals (IE(Li)>IE(Na)>IE(K)). In comparison with
CQD
32
, IEs are increased, reflecting energetical stabilization of the outer valence orbitals upon complexation, whereas EAs are decreased. Consequently, IE-EA, or the hardness parameter
η
for the open-shell complexes are increased by [1.8 eV (K)–2.1 eV (Li)] when compared with the H-L gap of [
CQD
32
], which is an indication of significant changes on the electronic structure of CQDs due to the interactions with metal species.

The main features in the electronic absorption spectra of CQDs predicted by TDDFT are in line with experimental works on CQDs and related systems. The calculations of the spectra support the view that absorption in the visible region is enhanced by CQD-M interactions. Moreover, in keeping with previous indications from experimental work our calculations provide further evidence on the important role played by CT excitations induced by CQD-M interactions.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
